title: conda
text:
The first step is to create a conda environment, containing the necessary tools for the required analysis. In this workflow, we use the snakemake package to help running the workflow, as well as some mappers. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
Environments can be created with the help of a file, e.g. environment.yaml using the following command

conda env create --name Project0815 --file environment.yaml

This file would look like that:
name: Project0815
channels:
   - bioconda
   - conda-forge
   - r
   - bioinfo
dependencies:
   - snakemake-minimal =5.2.4
   - python =3.6
   - star =2.7.2c
   - samtools =1.9
   - bowtie2 =2.3.5
   - bwa =0.7.17
The first row of the file contains the name of the project, i.e. the name of the conda environment created. Next are the channels listed, from which the conda environment should look for the tools needed for the analysis.
the last part of the environment file is a list of the needed dependencies. Each one of them has its version attached to it. This can be very helpful, when a data set needs to be re-analyzed to check for problems or conflicts with older/newer results or for debugging purposes. In this workflow we will use the snakemake package as well as python version 3.6. we also install here the mappers star, bwa and bowtie2. WE also need the samtool package for the manipulation of the bam files (sorting, filtering, etc.).
After this was done one must activate the environment using the command

source activate Project0815

If one look at the command line one can now see this
(Project0815) USER@SERVERNAME:Project0815$ ..
The brackets on the left side must contains the name of the environment.
If, after the environment was created, new tools must be added, they can just be added to the list of tools in the environment.yaml file and than the conda environment must be updated, using the command

conda env update -f environment.yaml 

The conda environment should be created only once, but with each login one must activate it, so that the snakemake script can be run from the correct paths.

title: conda
text:
The first step is to create a conda environment, containing the necessary tools for the required analysis. In this workflow, we use the snakemake package to help running the workflow, as well as some mappers. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
Environments can be created with the help of a file, e.g. environment.yaml using the following command

conda env create --name Project0815 --file environment.yaml

This file would look like that:
name: Project0815
channels:
   - bioconda
   - conda-forge
   - r
   - bioinfo
dependencies:
   - snakemake-minimal =5.2.4
   - python =3.6
   - star =2.7.2c
   - samtools =1.9
   - bowtie2 =2.3.5

The first row of the file contains the name of the project, i.e. the name of the conda environment created. Next are the channels listed, from which the conda environment should look for the tools needed for the analysis.
the last part of the environment file is a list of the needed dependencies. Each one of them has its version attached to it. This can be very helpful, when a data set needs to be re-analyzed to check for problems or conflicts with older/newer results or for debugging purposes. In this workflow we will use the snakemake package as well as python version 3.6. we also install here the mappers star, bwa and bowtie2. WE also need the samtool package for the manipulation of the bam files (sorting, filtering, etc.).
After this was done one must activate the environment using the command

source activate Project0815

If one look at the command line one can now see this
(Project0815) USER@SERVERNAME:Project0815$ ..
The brackets on the left side must contains the name of the environment.

The conda environment should be created only once, but with each login one must activate it, so that the snakemake script can be run from the correct paths.

title: snakemake
text:
This part is still work in progress.
snakemake relies heavily on a config file, i.e. config.yaml, where parameter specific for each analysis are saved. This reduces the number of changes needed to be done each time a new project is run.
For this project we are using the following config file: config.yaml
The config.yaml file contains several parts.

The top one contain the information about the organism used in the analysis. This is necessary as it provides the prefix for both the genome indexing as well as the mapping steps.


The config file contain seven different organisms and should be run only once at the beginning to create the indexed genomes. It must be ran again only when new organisms are added or new versions of any genomes (or of tools) are needed. For each genome the prefix would be the three-letter name of the organism followed by the genome built version (e.g Hsp.GRCh38, Cel.WBcel2358).

Below the Organism’s name are the links for the genomic sequence in a fastA format and the annotation files in a gtf (gene transfer file) format. On each first time both files will be downloaded before the genome indexing.

The next part contains parameters for the STAR aligner. In this section the parameters can be set globally and be easily called upon, when running multiple versions of the same aligner.

The most important parameter is gzipped. This set whether or not the sample files are compressed or not. STAR uses an internal decompressing step on the fly, when running the mapping step. As the fastq files from the core facility are usually gzipped, the default here is set to true.
The SA index set the preindex string. Bigger genomes take higher values. It should in general be set to \(~log2(GenomeLength)/2-1\). For standard genomes ( hsa or mmu) it should be set to 14, for smaller genomes such as Drosophila, it should be set to 13. C. elegans genome should be even smaller and be setted at 12. The higher the value, the faster the index building, but higher is also the memory requirements.
The memory can be set with the RAM parameter.

The third part of the config file is project-related and must be changed with each run. It has three parts:

Project number (e.g. P000)
Path to concatenated fastq files (e.g. /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/).
the organism to be mapped against (e.g. Cel.WBcel235).

This parameters must be changed each time a new run is done. The run will create a new folder within this working directory, where it will save the mapping results in form of sorted bam files and the bigwig files for visualiztion purposes.

This config file as well as the snakemake scripts are all placed in the scripts/ folder and should stay there. The run command will be set to find them there.
The snakemake workflow is based on so-called rules. This rules are a way to split the workflow into single steps. The advantage here, is that each time the steps can be ran separately. If some parameters are changed to only one step in the analysis snakemake can figure out which steps must be re-run and which one don’t.
This snakemake protocol works in two steps.
1. Getting the genome and indexing
2. Mapping the samples with the needed mapper and genome
The first more general step is to set the working directory. It would be best, if we use as less copying/moving of files as possible. For that reason it might be best, if we can directly work inside the conc.fastq folder, where the data is created after the demultiplxing. If so, some of the paths in the work flow would have to be changed or maybe adding a global work.dir parameter to the config.yaml file. (work.dir parameter in config.yaml file must be set).

Getting the genome and indexing
This work is pending the changes when the indexed genomes will be saved on the server permanently

Should we have a folder with several genomes already indexed and prepared for the mapping beforehand?
if so which genomes?
where should they be saved? The paths to the indexed genomes would than needed to be changed accordingly in the second snakemake file for the mapping step.

The advantage is, that if we have this done, this first script can be spared.
Getting and indexing the genome
In General the script uses the link and download both the genome and the annotation files for the given genome. it than automatically and in parallel indexes the genome for the three mappers - star, bwa and bowtie2.
CHANGES in version 3
In version 3 of the script, the rule get_genome was changed to accommodate the automatic download of multiple genomes. For that reason, wildcards were added as params, one for the fastA file and one for the gtf file. This is because snakemake’s brackets markup can only replace a variable with its string representation, but do not evaluate any codes. so first a string should be set to be passed over to the wget command in the shell.


Mapping the samples with the STAR
mapping the samples in a paired-end format
This script contains three (four) steps. in the first step the fastq samples are mapped to the indexed genome. Then, they are being indexed. Last, after creating a chromosome-size file, the bam files are converted into bigwig files for better visualization using the UCSC browser.
We have two separate scripts for single-end and paired-end runs. The PE run looks for both R1 and R2 fastq files, while the SE script will ignore all R2 samples.
After the environment was created and activated, one must change to the directory of the concatenated fast files. E.g.
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
To run the script use this command to test/run. The settings are explained below.
# for single-end samples
snakemake -nps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
# for paired-end samples
snakemake -ps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50

-n - dry-run. This parameter will only output the command, but execute them. This should be used to test the parameters, but must be removed for the analysis to be done.
-p - Print the commands to be execute.
-s - the name of the snakeake file to be executed.
-j - #cores. How many cores should be used in the run.

title: index
text:
The repository contains a conda-snakemake workflow for the mapping of the concatenated fastq file created at the BCF of the MPIB.
This README file for the sequencing center to help them run a preliminary analysis on the sequenced samples
The file will contains instruction on how to create a conda environment with the needed tools for the mapping and will also include snakemake for the automation of the scripts.

title: index
text:
steps to do
Preparing the genomes for mapping

Download the genomes and the annotations from the FTP server (Ensembl genome builds are used here.)
For each genome, an index for the star aligner is created.

This two steps should be done only once, when the workflow was set up. It only needs to be re-run, when a new organism is being added, or when newer genome build of exiting organisms or a newer version of the STAR mapping tool are needed.
Mapping step

Map the samples with the needed parameter.
Index the fastA file to create a chromSize file for the bigwig conversion.
Convert the bam files to wig and then to bigwig.

points to consider: If this is only a preliminary analysis -

Would it make more sense to take only 50% of the fastq file for a faster analysis / visualization
Do they need the bam files also - deleting them would save a lot of storage space

The examples in this document are based on fictive data as well as a fictional project named Project0815. When a new project is done, this term must be changed with the proper name of the project, wherever it is mentioned.

title: index
text:
folder structure
The workflow works from within the folder of the concatenated fastq files created after running the bcl2fastq script from the project management website (in the core facility here, this folder is called conc.fastq).

A config file is saved under /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/config.yaml, where all the mapping and genome parameters are saved.
The genome indexing step will run only once and creates a folder named genomes/ on the /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/. Within this folder all the fastA and the gtf files are saved as well as a folder for each organism, containing the star-indexed files.
The mapping step expect to find within the working directory the files with the pattern .conc.R1.fastq.gz, as this is the current pattern given by the sequencing facility. (If, in the future, this will be changed, the search pattern must be changed as well).

There are two separate files for single-ended and paired-ended data sets. The single-ended one expects to find only .conc.R1.fastq.gz, while the paired-end data set must also contain .conc.R2.fastq.gz files.

title: index
text:
Workflow
The workflow uses a combination of a conda environment with the snakemake tool (based on python). The advantage of conda is that it creates a container, separated from the rest of the tools installed on the system. This gives a better management and control over the tools used in the workflow, as well as less conflict potentials with other/older version of tools with non-compatible parameters. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
snakemake offers a lot of advantages as well. It allows better controls over different steps of the analysis, easier automation of the workflow and reduce running time through parallelization of the steps, just to mention some of them. to learn more about snakemake take a look at the snakemake doc site.

conda
snakemake


wigToBigwig
First the bam files are converted into wig files. This is done using the bam2wig.py script from the rseqc-software package.
In order for the automatic conversion of wig to bigwig files to work, one must have the tool wigToBigwig from the UCSC repository installed and in the path. As we are working with conda, the easiest place to put it is the the path of the environment. In this case, as we are working with miniconda the path would be under /fs/home/USERNAME/miniconda3/envs/ENVNAME/bin/. So far I have copied the file manually to the folder, but I have now added it to the environment.yaml file, which is ran during the environment creation step. This was not tested yet!.
TO DO
In this case it would probably be better if the indexed genomes would already be saved on the server for various organisms, as it doesn’t make sense to download one each time. We sort of know which organisms are needed in most cases.

human
mouse
zebra fish
fruit fly
yeast
worm (C. elegans)

as well as which mappers we need

star
bwa
bowtie2

title: Updating genomes and indices
text: This page collects the internal procedures for:

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
When to update genomes / indices
Once every few years the genomic sequences must be updated. Also the STAR version for mapping progresses and should be updated when needed.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
Prerequisites / where to run
You can use any server as long as you can activate the conda environment with snakemake, STAR, samtools, rseqc, and ucsc-wigtobigwig.

hpcl5002 (example)
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/
conda activate GenomeIndexing


ngs-vm (example)
Example conda environment creation:
conda create -n Mapping -c bioconda -c conda-forge snakemake-minimal star samtools rseqc ucsc-wigtobigwig

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
1) Update FASTA and GTF links
The config file used for genome indexing and mapping is config.yaml (in the pool-bcfngs folder). It contains a list of fasta and gtf links for each genome.
When a genome build is updated, update:

the fasta / gtf links
the organism prefix (folder name used for mapping), if needed

Example (D. melanogaster):
Dme.BDGP6.22:
  fasta: "ftp://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/current_fasta/drosophila_melanogaster/dna/Drosophila_melanogaster.BDGP6.22.dna.toplevel.fa.gz"
  gtf: "ftp://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/current_gtf/drosophila_melanogaster/Drosophila_melanogaster.BDGP6.22.91.gtf.gz"
  SAindex: 12
to:
Dme.BDGP6.28:
  fasta: "ftp://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/current_fasta/drosophila_melanogaster/dna/Drosophila_melanogaster.BDGP6.28.dna.toplevel.fa.gz"
  gtf: "ftp://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/current_gtf/drosophila_melanogaster/Drosophila_melanogaster.BDGP6.28.102.gtf.gz"
  SAindex: 12

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
2) Re-run genome indexing (STAR)
This step needs to be run only once (unless new genomes are added or indices are rebuilt for a new STAR version).

Log in to the server.
Activate the conda environment inside a screen session.
Edit the config file (example):

vi /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/config.yaml

Dry-run:

snakemake -nps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile

Run for real (example with 12 cores):

snakemake -ps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile -j 12

title: Updating genomes and indices
section: 3) Switch the active STAR index (symlink approach)
text:
3) Switch the active STAR index (symlink approach)
Downstream mapping often expects the index at:
/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/{organism}/starIndex/
If you build STAR indices into versioned folders, keep the stable path by updating a symlink named starIndex.
Example loop (adjust STAR version folder name):
folders="Cel.WBcel235
Dme.BDGP6.28
Dps.3.0.49
Dre.GRCz11
Eco.HUSEC2011CHR1
Hsp.GRCh38
Mmu.GrCm38
Sce.R64-1-1"

for folder in $folders
do
  echo $folder
  cd ${folder}/
  rm starIndex
  ln -s starIndex2.7.10a/ starIndex
  cd ../
done

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
4) Mapping (per project)
Mapping is done with STAR for a specific genome in either paired-end or single-end mode.

Change parameters before mapping
Adjust the project settings in the config file before running:
project: "P152"
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P146/P152_ChIP_Asx_5_6/conc.fastq"
org: "Dme.BDGP6.22"


Run mapping (dry-run first)
Change into the project folder containing the concatenated FASTQ files:
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
Dry-run then execute:
# single-end
snakemake -nps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50

# paired-end
snakemake -nps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
Remove -n to run for real.

title: HOWTO
text: This is an internal HOW-TO for the workflow of the automation for the bcfngs core facility.

title: HOWTO
text:
genome indexing
This steps needs to be run only once. unless new genomes are added.
This is all run on the hpcl5002.

log-in to the server.
If not done before, activate the conda environment inside a screen, so that one can log out from the server and still run the workflow in the background. More information about the is on the github web site.
open the config.yaml file, e.g. vi /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/config.yaml and add the genome links from the ensembl FTP site in this format:

  Dps.3.0.45:
    fasta: "ftp://ftp.ensemblgenomes.org/pub/metazoa/release-45/fasta/drosophila_pseudoobscura/dna/Drosophila_pseudoobscura.Dpse_3.0.dna.toplevel.fa.gz"
    gtf: "ftp://ftp.ensemblgenomes.org/pub/metazoa/release-45/gtf/drosophila_pseudoobscura/Drosophila_pseudoobscura.Dpse_3.0.45.gtf.gz"

run the command snakemake -nps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile to check that the new genome is added to the workflow (the -n is a dry-run which just output the command without performing the tasks.)
run the same command without the -n - snakemake -ps ../scripts/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile -j 12. This will download the fastA and gtf files from the given links and create the index files for them as well. The parameter -j sets the number pf cores used in the snakemake run. If the number of cores is smaller then the one sets for e.g. STAR, only this numner of cores will be used.

title: HOWTO
text:
Mapping
Mapping will be done with the STAR aligner for a specific genome either in a paired-end or single-end format.

Changing parameters before mapping
The following setting must be adjusted before running the script:
######## Project Setting
# To create a separate project results folder for each run, change the name of the project here.
project: "P152"
# this path would point to the raw data in fastq format. This must be changed each time for the correct data to be analyzed.
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P146/P152_ChIP_Asx_5_6/conc.fastq"
# Which organism to map the data to
org: "Dme.BDGP6.22"
The project and the path should point to the current project which is being mapped.
the org must be the organism the data is mapped against. The term must be identical to the term in the list of organisms from the config files.


running the star mapping step.
first, change the working directory to the path of the actual project on the pool-bcfngs. e.g.
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
Next run the snakemake script first as a dry-run (-n) and if all seems well than run it for real.
e.g. 
# for single-end samples
snakemake -nps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
# for paired-end samples
snakemake -ps /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/scripts/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
